next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

Nate got to Eastbridge Plaza a little before seven, early enough that the police cruiser had its light bar off and the orange barrels were still spaced loose and crooked, more setup than closure. The strip mall sat behind a fresh run of chain-link panels and plastic mesh, all of it silver and fluorescent against the gray morning. The windows had been emptied. Paper darkened the insides. A loose strand of Christmas tinsel skated along the curb in the wind and snagged on one of the sawhorses.

No one from Kassos had told him to be there. There was nothing for him to sign, nothing for him to inspect. Demolition belonged to other men now, men in insulated coveralls and hard hats with coffee cooling in their trucks. His part had been two weeks earlier in Victor's conference room with a legal pad under his hand. The pen had moved. That was all. He had kept his job. The rest of it only needed machines.

He parked across the street because there was nowhere else he could stand. He could not stand with the crew or with the people who'd lost the place. The Honda's heater took too long to matter. He shut it off after a minute because the fan made the windshield smell like hot dust and old pennies, and he didn't want one more machine in his ears.

The stores looked smaller with the signs still up and the insides gone. Gail's Diner had its red script over the awning, but the windows below it were blanked out with butcher paper. The barber pole outside Al's shop had been unplugged and stood there dead and striped. Lazar's Hardware still had the hand-painted sign on the end...
open_hooks_to_carry: The demolition meeting: timeline finalized, paperwork queued, the machinery in its last phase
Nate alone with the three options: refuse and lose everything, sign and participate, or warn Mort to no effect
prev_tail_excerpt:
"That's somehow worse."

"It's definitely sadder."

He rubbed his thumb over the condensation ring his glass had left on the bank statement until the paper started to pill.

"I'm so tired," he said, and heard how flat it came out.

Sarah looked at him for a second. Not pitying. Just looking.

"I know," she said.

They sat there until both glasses were empty. Sarah turned one page of the essay, then turned it back, red pen resting across her knuckles.

When Sarah finally stood, she gathered the essay and the red pen from the counter, frowned at the smear on her hand, and headed back toward the bedroom. At the hall she stopped.

"If he calls tomorrow," she said without turning around, "ask him the name of the place. Not the guys. The actual place."

"Why."

"Because if he won't say it, he's still going."

Nate nodded even though she could not see it.

"OK."

She disappeared into the dark.

Nate sat alone a little longer with the checkbook, the statement, and the ugly clean space where the cushion had been. Outside, somewhere down on the street, a man laughed too loudly and then coughed until the laugh became work. Nate finished the last swallow in his glass and looked at the numbers one more time. Looking away would have felt like optimism, and he was done with that for the night.
state_deltas_required: The demolition twelve days away, all internal options exhausted, and the crushing recognition that Nate faces the same impossible choice Dominic faced

Nate makes the same choice Dominic made — complicity over unemployment — and in doing so completes the novel's central transformation: he has become the man he killed, not through malice but through the same systemic pressure.
